mission.title: Install and Configure Kmesh on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kmesh is a high-performance service mesh data plane based on eBPF and programmable kernel, designed to enhance traffic management, security, and monitoring without requiring changes to application code. Installing Kmesh allows you to leverage its low overhead and sidecarless architecture for efficient service communication.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Kmesh Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kmesh https://github.com/kmesh-net/kmesh-helm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Kmesh using Helm
steps.description:
Install Kmesh in your Kubernetes cluster using the Helm chart. Replace `<namespace>` with your desired namespace:
```bash
helm install kmesh kmesh/kmesh-helm --namespace <namespace> --create-namespace --version 0.1.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Kmesh pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Update the Kmesh deployment to set resource limits for the Kmesh daemon. You can do this by editing the values.yaml file or directly using kubectl:
```bash
kubectl patch deployment kmesh --namespace <namespace> -p '{"spec":{"template":{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"kmesh","resources":{"limits":{"cpu":"1","memory":"800Mi"}}}]}}}}'
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring
steps.description:
Enable monitoring features by modifying the Kmesh configuration. You can edit the Helm values or use kubectl to apply changes:
```bash
kubectl edit configmap kmesh-config --namespace <namespace>
# Add or modify the monitoring settings in the config map as needed.
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Kmesh will show the Kmesh pods in a running state when you execute `kubectl get pods`. Additionally, the resource limits and monitoring configurations should be applied correctly to ensure optimal performance.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kmesh https://github.com/kmesh-net/kmesh-helm
helm repo update

kubectl edit configmap kmesh-config --namespace <namespace>
# Add or modify the monitoring settings in the config map as needed.

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Kmesh release from your Kubernetes cluster using Helm. Replace `<namespace>` with your desired namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall kmesh --namespace <namespace>
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent resources associated with Kmesh:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep kmesh | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace used for Kmesh and verify that it has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <namespace>
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current Kmesh configuration and resources:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace <namespace> -o yaml > kmesh-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Kmesh installation to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade kmesh kmesh/kmesh-helm --namespace <namespace> --version 0.1.1
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Kmesh pods are running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace <namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If Kmesh pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace <namespace>
```
Look for any error messages that indicate configuration issues or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If services are not reachable, verify that the Kmesh configuration is correct:
```bash
kubectl get configmap kmesh-config --namespace <namespace> -o yaml
```
Ensure that the service endpoints are properly defined.

troubleshooting.title: High resource usage
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high CPU or memory usage, check the resource limits set for Kmesh:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment kmesh --namespace <namespace>
```
Consider adjusting the resource limits in the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails, check the Helm release status:
```bash
helm status kmesh --namespace <namespace>
```
If necessary, roll back to the previous version:
```bash
helm rollback kmesh --namespace <namespace> <revision-number>
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
